text: Ethnic minorities have played an out-sized role in historical and contemporary development. Examples abound of how small immigrant communities, both historically and in the present, have spurred on economic development by bringing innovation, and skills and complementing the skill sets of the native population. Yet, quantitative evidence is limited and the mechanisms remain poorly understood, especially in developing countries where the long-term impacts may differ greatly. In this paper, we seek to shed light on this question by exploring the role of Chinese minority communities in long-term economic development in Java, a business community that, globally, has been called one of the most powerful economic forces in the world ( Redding , 2013 ). Ethnic Chinese immigration into Indonesia began as early as the 13th century. These earlier waves were often a result of poorer individuals escaping from the lack of cultivable land, wars, and famines in southern China, primarily what is today the provinces of Guangdong and Fujian. In 1407, Zheng He’s first expeditions mostly landed on the northern shore of Central Java around today’s Semarang, before moving on to various settlements on the now-defunct Muria strait and then larger settlements in Eastern Java. While some areas in Eastern Java already had some Chinese communities before, the ones around Semarang and the Muria strait were mostly populated with Chinese after Zheng He’s expeditions, including a fraction of the crew that decided to stay and build a Chinese community in Semarang. Over the subsequent six centuries, waves of Chinese immigrants continued to settle across Java. While Chinese communities likely promoted long-term economic development, the observed association between Chinese communities and economic development can also be driven by sorting. Specifically, if settlers sought locations with particularly favorable fundamentals, or were forced to settle more marginal land, this could explain any correlation between the size of a Chinese community and economic development. To address this issue, we use an instrumental variable strategy motivated by features of Chinese settlement in Indonesia. The size of contemporary communities is shaped by historical settlements, which were in part driven by the choice of Chinese maritime portage sites on Java’s northern coast. Many of these portage sites, chosen based on pre-industrial maritime connectivity with limited information about the hinterland’s economic potential, have since become inaccessible from the sea due to shifts in the location of the coastline. As a result, their influence on contemporary economic development likely stems from their role

in shaping the location of Chinese communities. We challenge this assumption in several ways and provide evidence in support of this interpretation below. Furthermore, we adjust for different exposure to the portage sites driven by geography by implementing the procedure developed in Borusyak and Hull ( 2023 ). We find a strong, precisely estimated impact of distance to the Muria strait on the size of the Chinese communities, as measured as the share of the population belonging to a Chinese community, across districts in 1930. Specifically, an increase in 1km distance to the Muria strait leads to a decrease in ethnic Chinese shares of about 9 percentage points in the preferred specification. Reassuringly, the association between distance to the strait is robust to controlling for differences in observable location fundamentals. We next use the distance to the strait, adjusted for average exposure consistent with Borusyak and Hull ( 2023 ), as an instrument for the size of the Chinese community. We begin by exploring the impact of the size of the Chinese community by district on contemporary economic development. We find that a one percentage point increase in the size of the Chinese community leads to an around 7 . 6 percent increase in average household consumption today. Furthermore, we also find a 21 . 1 percent increase in the population density in 2000. We next explore the impact on nightlight luminosity following Henderson et al. ( 2012 ). Consistent with the previous results, we find a positive impact on nightlight luminosity. In particular, a one percentage point increase in 1930 Chinese shares leads to a 3 . 04 increase in night-time light intensity between 1992 and 2010. Last, we find strong evidence of structural transformation. Districts with 1 percentage point higher 1930 Chinese shares have 8.9 (6.1) percentage points lower (higher) employment share in agriculture (services). Taken together, the findings suggest a positive impact of the size of the Chinese community on contemporary economic development. Why are districts with larger Chinese communities more economically developed? We consider several possibilities. Motivated by the literature we consider several mechanisms such as investment in human capital (see e.g. Rocha et al. , 2017 ; Droller , 2018 ; Gonz ́alez , 2020 ), the transmission of an entrepreneurial or market-oriented culture ( Figlio et al. , 2019 ), access to commercial networks and trade facilitated by Chinese communities ( Johnson and Koyama , 2017 ). Exploring the effect of Chinese community size on sectoral employment, we find evidence of larger employment shares in small-scale commerce in the services sector. Taken together, these findings provide evidence that

Chinese communities have had a positive impact on economic development, possibly through the diffusion and establishment of cultural practices promoting market participation. This paper contributes to several literatures. First, we contribute to a growing literature on the effects of historical immigration on long-run development. Previous studies have highlighted the importance of human capital in shaping immigrants’ effects on economic development ( Rocha et al. , 2017 ; Droller , 2018 ; Gonz ́alez , 2020 ; Burchardi et al. , 2020 ; Sequeira et al. , 2020 ). Notably, Burchardi et al. ( 2020 ) which finds that immigration into the US has had a net positive impact on development over a 40-year period, driven by highly educated innovators. Sequeira et al. ( 2020 ) show that lowskilled immigration in the US had positive effects in the early stages of industrialization. This paper focuses on a single minority group in a developing country and shows how low-skilled immigrants can drive local development through capital accumulation and local, social commercial networks and practices. In this sense, our findings also speak to evidence of the role of migrant communities in the diffusion of cultural practices and norms ( Valencia , 2019 ; Dippel and Heblich , 2021 ). Our work also relates to a rich literature on the long-term economic effects of ethnic and religious minorities ( Jha , 2013 ; Hornung , 2014 ; Johnson and Koyama , 2017 ). There are notable parallels between the economic roles of Jewish communities in Europe and Chinese communities in Southeast Asia. In this regard, our paper builds on Johnson and Koyama ( 2017 ) who study the role of Jewish communities in promoting economic development in pre-industrial Europe. Our paper differs in emphasizing the long-term impact beyond the pre-industrial context. Methodologically, our paper also builds on Johnson and Koyama ( 2017 ) as Chinese settlement followed maritime trading routes, unlike the overland routes followed by Jewish communities in Europe. Finally, and more broadly, our paper contributes to the quantitative literature on determinants of long-term economic development in Indonesia (see e.g. van der Eng , 1992 , 2010 ; Dell and Olken , 2020 ). 1 The paper proceeds as follows. Section 2 discusses key aspects of various waves of ethnic Chinese out-migration to Indonesia starting from the 15th century till the early 20th century. We especially highlight those relevant to our identification strategy. Section 3 describes our data sources. Section 4 outlines our empirical strategy. Section 5.1 describes our main results and robustness. Section 6 explores the mechanisms. Section 7 concludes. 1 There is a small related literature on economic history on Indonesia. In particular, Hup and de Zwart ( 2024 ) studies changes in wages of Chinese emigrants to Indonesia in the late 19th to early 20th century.

section_name: Historical Background: Chinese Immigration to Indonesia
text: This section describes the background of the historical immigration of ethnic Chinese into Indonesia over six centuries. In particular, we focus on three key facts that inform our main identification strategy: (1) Pre-15th century immigration; (2) The (lack of) choice over portage sites in the early 15th century that led to plausibly exogenous initial ethnic Chinese settlement patterns; (3) Post-15th century immigration. 2 First, Chinese emigration to Java before the 15th century was highly limited. 3 While evidence of Chinese immigrants can be traced as far back as the 13th century, there is little written record of their presence throughout most of Java. Based on the only written account of Zheng He’s expeditions in the 1400s by a member of the crew, Ma Huan (Ma Huan 1433, translated by Mills, 1970), the expeditions only encountered some Chinese on shore in Turban and Gresik in Easter Java. Second, the appearance of large ethnic Chinese settlements on Java can be largely traced to Zheng He’s voyage into the Dutch East Indies (as Indonesia was then called) in the early 15th century. Zheng He commanded one of the largest sea fleets in the world in his multiple voyages from China down to various parts of Southeast Asia. His travels are extensively documented in primary archival sources. In particular, contemporary ethnic Chinese settlement today can be traced to many of the portage sites along the north coast of Java where members of his crew and voyage landed. Figure 1 plots 1930 ethnic Chinese shares and, with a solid black line, Zheng He’s sea passage along the historical north coast of Java, the Muria Straits . These portage sites are based on historical records that record the passage of Zheng He’s fleet through the historical towns of Semarang, Demak, Koedoes, Pati, Rembang and Lasem, as portage sites. Importantly, the historiography suggests that these sites were unlikely to have been selected based on economic or locational fundamentals – seafarers then had little recourse to modern-day navigational equipment and Zheng He’s fleet was visiting a hitherto unknown land. Furthermore and crucially, all of these portage sites are, today, in-accessible by water due to extensive silting from Mount Muria in 1657. This allays concerns that advances and changes in latter-day maritime trade and migration could be omitted factors driving any results we find on ethnic Chinese shares. Third, the first main influx of post-15th century Chinese immigrants arose with the fall of the 2 This section is largely based off Suryadinata ( 2005 ) unless stated otherwise. 3 The discussion in this page draws extensively from Hoadley ( 1988 ) and Carey ( 1984 )

section_name: Historical Background: Chinese Immigration to Indonesia
text: Ming dynasty (1644) and the reopening of Chinese trade with Southeast Asia in 1683. This created ideal conditions for an increased flow of immigrants from the main southern maritime provinces like Hokkiens from Amoy in Fukien province and Kwong Fus (Cantonese) from Canton and Macau. Many Chinese assimilated very closely with the indigenous population, married local women, and adopted local customs and religions like Islam. An important reason was that virtually no Chinese women migrated overseas before the late 19th century. A possible second main influx of Chinese immigrants to Indonesia arose near the end of the fall of the Qing dynasty (1644 - 1911) where many ethnic Chinese fled to Southeast Asia due to famine and war. Crucially, kinship ties and pre-existing clan networks in Southeast Asia were the key determinants of where latter-day Chinese immigrants chose to settle ( Setijadi , 2023 ). Hence, we see in our data that initial portage sites in the early 15th-century, plausibly unrelated to modern-day locational fundamentals, have ethnic Chinese communities that are disproportionate in both size and share relative to 19th-century shipping and aviation routes.

section_name: Historical Background: Chinese Immigration to Indonesia
text: The Historical Role of Ethnic Chinese in Indonesia Chinese migrants have played important economic and political roles. These included that of retailers, tax-collectors, tax-farmers as well as middlemen between Javanese princes and Dutch officials during the Dutch colonial period. At the extreme, a number of Chinese were even appointed as tumenggung , or retainers in the Islamic courts, and were assigned vassals and rice fields for support. Many of these Chinese took on local names and titles. The importance of the Chinese in pivotal economic roles can be seen in the following quotes. First, most tax collectors and farmers were Chinese. Along the north coast of Java, van Hoorn wrote that ”there is not a river, harbor, bay, or creek navigable for shipping, which does not have a customs post (tollgate), of which the keeper is invariably a Chinese.” There are also indications that whole provinces of the Mataram empire were farmed by the Chinese during this period. For example, the fertile, rice-producing areas of Pekalongan and Batang, amounting to 20 percent of the entire coastal population in 1704, were leased by the Sunan (provincial ruler) to a Chinese family who ruled under Javanese names. The Chinese were also active as retailers in the profitable opium and textile traders, middlemen handling local commerce in raw cotton, salt, indigo, tobacco, ginger, silks, and other important commodities. Many eventually rose to become entrepreneurs and large traders who played important roles in developing Indonesia’s nascent rice and sugar growing industries. Furthermore,

section_name: Historical Background: Chinese Immigration to Indonesia
text: Chinese merchants came to play a crucial in informal capital markets already in the early 19th century ( Zanden and Marks , 2012 , p. 32).

section_name: Historical Background: Chinese Immigration to Indonesia
text: The Contemporary Role of Ethnic Chinese in Indonesia Ethnic Chinese businesses have been called one of the most powerful economic forces in the world ( Redding , 2013 ). In particular, Indonesia is the largest country in Southeast Asia and is the single country with the largest absolute number of ethnic Chinese ( Suryadinata , 2008 ). In terms of shares, however, the ethnic Chinese are one of the smallest ethnic groups in Indonesia, with estimates ranging from 1.5 to 2 percent of the total Indonesian population of 230 million. Despite their small share, however, the ethnic Chinese in Indonesia have played a significant economic role. For example, the share of total private domestic capital owned by ethnic Chinese exceeds that of any other ethnic group in Indonesia ( Efferin and Pontjoharyo , 2006 ).

In this section, we describe our main data sources. We utilize various sources of rich individual and firm-level data at various points from the early 20th to early 21st century. First, we construct our main explanatory variable of district-level ethnic Chinese shares from the Indonesian Population Census (1930, 2000, 2010). We also construct individual-level economic sector participation rates from the 2010 Census. Second, we construct measures of firm-level profits, sales, and productivity from the Indonesian Manufacturing Census ( Statistik Industri ) (1980-2006). 4 Third, we construct measures of village-level household consumption, trust, and attitudes towards the state from the Indonesian Socio-Economic Surveys ( Susenas 2000-2012). Fourth, nightlight intensity from NGHIS. Fifth, village census data ( PODES ).

Indonesian Population Census. First, we construct ethnic Chinese shares from 3 rounds of the Indonesian Population Census: 1930, 2000, and 2010. The 1930 census is uniquely suited for our purposes. It was the first to provide a complete enumeration of all Indonesians of Chinese descent at the municipal and sub-district level. We use this to construct our main explanatory variable, Chinese Shares , and for estimation purposes, aggregate this measure to the district level. Notably, 4 We supplement this with two rounds of the Indonesian Firm Census in 2006 and 2016.

the 2000 census is the first census round, since 1930, in which individual-level ethnicity is recorded – all questions on ethnicity were banned under Suharto (1965-1999). 5 Second, we construct measures of employment shares in various economic sectors using the 2010 Population Census. Crucially, these sectors include trade, transportation, and finance which the ethnic Chinese specialized in ( Pepinsky , 2016 ). We estimate the effects on two levels. First, on aggregated shares in agricultural, manufacturing, and service. Second, on specific sectoral shares as informed by the historical and anthropological literature. Figure 1 plots the spatial distribution of ethnic Chinese communities, in terms of ethnic shares, from the 1930 census. We document the following key facts. First, the majority of Chinese are concentrated around the Northern coast of Java. Visually, the impact of the Muria Straits, outlined with a solid black line, on Chinese settlement patterns is clear. In Central Java, we see a high ethnic Chinese concentration along the Muria Straits, which decreases as we move further away. Importantly, these settlements are situated along a coastline that has not existed since the early 18th century. Yet, ethnic Chinese concentration continues to persist in the 1930s, underscoring the importance of initial settlement patterns in determining latter-day ethnic settlements. Second, and more generally, the spatial distribution reflects traditional maritime shipping routes driven by wind and steam which resulted in fewer Chinese settling on the southern coast and in the interior of Java.

Socio-Economic Surveys We are interested in estimating the impact of Chinese presence, be it through business establishments, credit, loans, or other mechanisms highlighted in the [previous section] , on the outcomes of local Indonesians of non-ethnic Chinese descent. Susenas covers a representative sample of households and individuals in each round. We pool all available rounds from 2000-2010 (following Dell & Olken 2020) to obtain household-level measures of consumption of local, non-Chinese individuals. Pooling all Susenas rounds, we construct our main measure of household-level income and welfare by taking the logarithmic of equivalent household consumption ( Deaton , 1997 ) between 2000-2010.

Village Census ( PODES ) This gives us measures of population density in 2000, we supplement this with population counts from the 2010 Population Census. 5 Having said that, we have found archival records of ethnic Chinese counts in the 1970s and are in the process of digitizing district-level measures of ethnic Chinese shares in 1970 as an additional robustness check.
Night-lights We use average night-time light intensity from NGHIS, aggregated to either the village or district-level polygons in 2000.

Manufacturing Census ( Statistik Indonesia ) We use all rounds from 1980-2006. The Manufacturing Census covers all firms that employ minimally 20 to 30 employees and is limited to firms involved in any form of manufacturing activity. Hence, we supplement this with the Economic Census in 2016 (described below). THe Manufacturing Census gives us measures of firm-level sales, profits, and productivity which we aggregate to the district level.

Economic Census 2016 We use the latest round of the Indonesian Economic Census. This dataset comprises the universe of small and medium firms in rural areas ( > = [20-30] employees) and a representative sample of similarly sized firms in urban areas. The key advantage of this dataset is that it surveys both manufacturing and non-manufacturing firms, and classifies these firms into 19 disaggregated economic sector categories. Firm-size measures here, however are largely limited to number of employees. Hence, we supplement this with various rounds of the Manufacturing Census ( Statistik Indonesia ).
We begin our exploration of the long-run association between the size of Chinese communities on contemporary economic development using the following regression model,

where Y pd is the level of economic development in province p and district d , and C pd denotes the size of the Chinese community in district d as measured by the share of Chinese of the total population in 1930 . X ′ pd includes observable location fundamentals such as agricultural productivity and proximity to large urban centers, that might have affected the formation of Chinese communities and economic development. δ p is a province-fixed effect that captures unobserved heterogeneity at the province level. Finally, ε pd is an unobserved error term, potentially correlated within provinces. In Equation 1 , δ approximates the average difference in economic development across districts with where the Chinese community differs in size, keeping unobserved heterogeneity at the

province level and observed location fundamentals fixed. Two broad factors influence the size of δ . First, differences in contemporary development can be caused by the communities through human capital, cultural persistence, or improved commercial linkages to China. Second, the observed association might be influenced by the sorting of settlers into locations that differ in unobserved characteristics. This could happen if Chinese immigrants could only settle more marginal land, with lower agricultural productivity. This might have stunted subsequent industrialization, and urbanization in districts with larger Chinese communities. Furthermore, classical measurement error could attenuate the estimated observed association between Chinese community size and contemporary development. To isolate the causal impact of the size of Chinese communities, we therefore implement an instrumental variable strategy that leverages two key features of Chinese settlement in Indonesia. Since the size of Chinese communities is highly persistent, contemporary settlement is driven by historical settlement which in turn was influenced by the choice of Chinese maritime portage sites on the Northern coast of Java. We hypothesize that the locations chosen by the initial pioneers in turn influenced subsequent migrants’ location choices. The locations of these portage sites were primarily driven by pre-industrial maritime connectivity and were chosen with limited information about the economic potential of the hinterland. We therefore assume that locations with varying distances to these portage sites are similar in unobserved characteristics. Importantly, due to soil erosion and resulting shifts in the location of the coastline, the initial portage sites have not been accessible by sea since the early 18th-century. As a result, the portage sites are unlikely to be important locations for trade or destinations for Chinese migration. Taken together, we assume that any long-term impact of proximity to these historical portage sites is mediated through its effect on the formation of Chinese communities. We consider the following model:

where the variables are defined as above. β now denotes the causal impact of changes in the Chinese community size on contemporary economic development in a district. To estimate β , we estimate the first stage relationship between the distance to the portage site and the size of the Chinese

where D d denotes the distance to the portage site for district d . Following the above discussion, we assume that the distance to the portage sites is unrelated to unobserved determinants of economic development and only affects contemporary development through its impact on the location of Chinese communities. We discuss the plausibility of these assumptions below. In Equation 2 , β captures the difference in economic development across locations with different distances to the historical Chinese portage locations, keeping unobserved determinants of contemporary economic development constant. However, given the localized nature of the shock, some locations would be more likely to be treated regardless of which segment of the coastline actually served as portage locations. Regardless of which segment of the coastline the portage site emerged, due to its central location, it would have had a higher average exposure to the shock under any treatment assignment. To address this concern, we follow Borusyak and Hull ( 2023 ) and calculate the expected treatment of each district that we denote E [ ln ( D pd )] where the expectation is taken over alternative treatment assignment procedures over all the potential portage sites. Specifically, for each coastal segment, we simulate the assignment process of Chinese portage sites and average the distance over all potential assignments. For each district, we then subtract the expected distance to a portage location from the realized distance. Figure 2 provides a visual depiction of this process and Section A.1 discusses this process in further detail. The resulting first stage specification is therefore given by ln ( C pd ) = θ p + η ln � D ′ pd  + X ′ d φ + υ dp , (4)
where D ′ pd = D pd − E [ D pd ] . Finally, we cluster standard errors at the 1930 Dutch Regency level. 6

This section presents our main results. Throughout, we report Kleibergen-Paap F-statistics and report all other relevant weak-instrument robust test statistics. We begin by estimating our first-stage 6 In the 1930s, Java was largely divided into the following administrative tiers. From the top: Residency, Regency, District, and Sub-District. Modern-day administrative tiers, from the top, are Province, District, and Sub-District. 1930 Regencies are roughly equivalent in size and importance to large modern-day districts.

results of distance to 15th-century portage sites on 1930 ethnic Chinese shares. We then investigate the effects of 1930 ethnic Chinese shares on (i) wealth and economic development, as measured by village-level household consumption, nightlight intensity, and district-level population density; (ii) structural transformation. In the subsequent section, we present results on key mechanisms including increases in district-level economic specialization in the financial and services sector using firm-level counts from the 2016 Economic Census and individual-level economic participation data from the full-count 2010 population census.

First-stage. Table 1 presents our first-stage results from estimating equation ( 2 ). Column (1) shows the raw correlation between ethnic Chinese shares in 1930 and our recentered IV. An increase in 1km distance leads to a decrease in ethnic Chinese shares of about 19.2pp. Column (2) shows our preferred specification: the correlation between ethnic Chinese shares in 1930 and our recentered IV, conditional on a battery of predetermined covariates. 7 The coefficient nearly halves in size but, reassuringly, remains statistically significant at the 1% significance level. Having said that, we note that the Kleibergen-Papp F-stat for our instrument in our later results falls slightly below 10 and hence we report additional weak-instrument robust test statistics throughout. In general, p-values for under-identification remain below 0.05.

Contemporary Development. Table 2 estimates our results on three measures of contemporary economic development. Column (1) estimates that a 1p.p. increase in 1930 ethnic Chinese shares leads to a 7.6% increase in average household consumption today. Similarly, Column (2) estimates that a 1p.p. increase in 1930 ethnic Chinese shares leads to a 21p.p. higher population density in 2000. Finally, Column (3) estimates the effects of 1930 Chinese shares on changes in night-time light intensity ( Henderson et al. , 2012 ) between 1992, the first year for which night-time light intensity data is available, and 2010. A 1p.p. increase in 1930 Chinese shares leads to a 3.04 increase in nighttime light intensity from 1992-2010. Compared to the mean, our estimates suggest that increases in nighttime light intensity, in districts with 1p.p. higher ethnic Chinese shares, was nearly a third higher. 7 These covariates are altitude, linear and squared polynomials of the natural logarithm of distance to coastlines, to Batavia (present-day Jakarta), to Semarang, Tuban, Gresik, Trowulan, Surabaya, the natural logarithm of total area (1930), total dry agricultural land (1963), and total wet agricultural land (1963) and longitude.

Structural Transformation. We continue by examining if the higher economic development levels documented above may have accelerated structural transformation (see e.g. Herrendorf et al. , 2014 ). Table 3 estimates results on employment shares in agricultural, manufacturing, and services in 2010. Columns (1), (2), and (3) show that a district with 1p.p. higher ethnic Chinese shares in 1930, has on average, 8.9p.p. fewer individuals working in the agricultural sector, 2.8p.p. more individuals working in the manufacturing sector, and 6.1p.p. more individuals working in the services sector. Taken together, our results suggest that districts with higher ethnic Chinese shares are, today, more economically developed with a higher (lower) share of individuals working in services (agricultural). Are there specific sectors within the service industry that experienced positive effects from increases in Chinese presence? We turn to this when exploring mechanisms in Section 6 .

Our results are robust to alternative specifications. In particular, Table A.1 shows that results on contemporary development are robust to the inclusion of West Java. Similarly, Table A.2 checks for the robustness of our results on structural transformation. Column (1) shows that estimates on agricultural employment shares remain negative and statistically significant. Columns (2) and (3), show that estimates on manufacturing and service employment shares remain positive but become statistically insignificant. This is possibly due to the weaker strength of our instrument and is expected given that growth in West Java is strongly driven by economic and locational fundamentals that have less to do with plausibly exogenous, historical, ethnic Chinese settlement. For example, Jakarta and the largest concentration of special economic zones in Indonesia, with large local and foreign investment, are located in both the interior and along the northern coast of West Java.

Historical ethnic Chinese settlement has positive and persistent effects on development today that can be observed in consumption, population density, and higher nightlight growth. What explains these persistent effects? The economic and historical literature suggests 3 main mechanisms through which Chinese communities could have led to higher economic development: (1) Human capital; (2) Cultural transmission; (3) Market access and trade; and (4) Economic specialization and
complementarities. We show estimates for our results on (4), and are in the process of collecting data to test mechanisms (1), (2), and (3).

Human capital. Squicciarini and Voigtl ̈ander ( 2015 ) and Gennaioli et al. ( 2013 ) argue that elite human capital may be particularly important for transmitting knowledge and ideas. Anecdotal sources suggest that contemporary human capital in Chinese communities is relatively higher but the situation regarding historical human capital is unclear. Relatedly, Rocha et al. ( 2017 ) documents the persistence of human capital from immigrants in Brazil with higher levels of schooling and shows that there is a positive effect on long-term development.

Cultural transmission. Figlio et al. ( 2019 ) look at the impact of the cultural transmission of a ”Long Term Orientation” mindset of American immigrants to their children on educational outcomes. Here, the question would be whether there was a transmission of a culture of entrepreneurship and hard work and the importance of these channels beyond that of human capital, to the natives.

Market access and trade. Johnson and Koyama ( 2017 ) show that cities with Jewish communities in pre-industrial Europe experienced greater benefits from an increase in market access across Europe between 1400 and 1850. Cities with Jewish communities were better able to take advantage of the intensive margin and translate greater market density into growth. Similarly, Chinese communities have played an out-sized role as tax-farmers, tax collectors, and entrepreneurs since the early 18th century ( Carey , 1984 ; Hoadley , 1988 ) which is suggestive of a similar explanation for the link between Chinese communities and economic growth in Indonesia.

Economic specialization and complementarities. A rich literature documents that the ethnic Chinese were often, from the mid-20th century onwards, excluded from both the military and politics ( Setijadi , 2023 ). This resulted in many ethnic Chinese remaining in their traditional roles of trade, finance, and commerce. Given this, we hypothesize that districts with higher ethnic Chinese shares might have experienced larger firm growth due to ethnic Chinese specialization in these sectors. In the Indonesian context, firm panel data does not exist. As a proxy, Column (1) of Table 4 regresses average firm sales, calculated by pooling all rounds of the Indonesian Manufacturing Census (1986-2006), on ethnic Chinese shares. We find that a 1p.p. increase in 1930 ethnic Chinese shares leads to a 29.1p.p. increase in firm sales. Column (2) regresses the number of financial firms

from the 2016 Manufacturing Census on ethnic Chinese shares and finds that districts with 1p.p. higher ethnic Chinese shares in 1930 have nearly 65p.p. more finance firms. Next, we turn to individual-level employment in various service sub-sectors that either have had a historical Chinese presence or are likely to be upstream and/or downstream to sectors where the ethnic Chinese have had a dominant presence. Table 5 , Columns (1)-(6), regresses the number of individuals employed in Trade, Food and Accommodation ( FoodAccomm ), Transport, Information Communication and Technology, Finance, and Educational, Health and Social Services ( EduHealthSoc ) at the village-level. We find that higher ethnic Chinese shares are positively associated with higher employment in all sub-sectors except for ICT and Finance. 8

This paper presents novel evidence of the effects of historical ethnic Chinese immigration and settlement on the trajectory of Indonesia’s long-run economic development. We show that the ethnic Chinese have played a central economic role, disproportionate to their numbers, and disparities in economic development across much of Java can be traced to differences in initial ethnic Chinese settlement patterns. These long-run differences show up, crucially, in non-ethnic Chinese villages and non-ethnic Chinese individual-level measures of economic welfare and prosperity throughout Java and we provide evidence that the Chinese, in line with much of the qualitative historical literature, played an important role in financing and middle-men trade of businesses that continues to be apparent today. The role of the ethnic Chinese in Indonesian development has been under-studied in much of the existing literature and our paper corrects this.
8 This is possibly because firms in ICT and Finance employ a smaller number of individuals on average.
